current_fact.fact_text: On March 31, 1995, Tejano superstar Selena Quintanilla-Pérez was murdered by Yolanda Saldívar, the president of her own fan club, in a Corpus Christi motel. At just 23, Selena was on the verge of crossing from Spanish‑language fame into global pop stardom, and her death turned her music into a powerful, enduring symbol of Latina identity and loss.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

current_fact.match_reason: An episode honoring transformative female voices is a natural home for remembering Selena, whose life and death deeply shaped Latin and global pop culture.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1901, the Czech National Opera House in Prague premiered Dvořák’s "Rusalka," a work that blended folk tales, lush orchestration, and the eerie pull of water worlds. While its composer was already famous for symphonies, this opera slowly became one of his most beloved pieces, carried by a moonlit aria that still ripples through concert halls.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

queue.match_reason: An aquatic fairy‑tale opera about a water spirit aligns with an episode themed around immersion, fluidity, and the sensation of moving below the surface.

queue.fact_text: On March 31, 1931, the Managua earthquake’s devastation was compounded by destructive fires that burned for days, destroying most of the city’s adobe and timber buildings. The rebuilding that followed changed not only Managua’s architecture but also how Nicaraguans thought about urban planning, hazard, and the trade‑off between tradition and safety.

episode.title: Morning Drops #25 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: The haunting, atmospheric textures of trip‑hop pair with the image of a city remade in the aftermath of quakes and fire—slow, cinematic, and layered with memory.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 2016, Scott Kelly’s year‑in‑space mission ended, but its most intriguing effects were only beginning to be understood as scientists compared his body to that of his Earthbound twin brother Mark. Early findings suggested changes in gene expression, immune responses, and even the length of chromosome‑protecting telomeres, turning one astronaut into a kind of living before‑and‑after snapshot of human adaptation.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: This follow‑up angle on the year‑long ISS mission focuses on biological learning and adaptation, neatly connecting to an episode about how change reshapes the brain over time.

queue.fact_text: On March 31, 1933, as part of the same New Deal wave that created the Civilian Conservation Corps, the U.S. also moved to reshape its relationship with landscapes through large public works and parks. Many trails, fire lookouts, and campgrounds built in this era became the backdrop for countless personal stories—holidays, friendships, and quiet mornings in nature that outlived the politics that started them.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

queue.match_reason: An era that built the physical infrastructure for future journeys into nature pairs with an episode lived out on the water, where shared travel turns landscapes into memories.

published.fact_text: In 2016, after orbiting Earth 5,440 times, NASA astronaut Scott Kelly and Roscosmos cosmonaut Mikhail Kornienko returned from a nearly year‑long mission aboard the International Space Station. Their extended stay turned their bodies into living experiments, helping scientists understand how microgravity, radiation, and isolation reshape the human body and mind over time.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A literal long-term "space shift" in human experience pairs naturally with an episode built around rethinking emptiness as creative space.

published.fact_text: On March 31, 1966, the Soviet Union launched Luna 10, which soon became the first spacecraft to orbit the Moon. The probe even carried a radio transmitter that relayed a specially arranged version of the Soviet anthem during its orbit, turning a piece of Cold War engineering into a kind of orbital performance.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

published.match_reason: The first evening-flow voyage mirrors Luna 10’s pioneering orbital journey: both are quiet, rule‑free explorations into uncharted space.

published.fact_text: In 2005, astronomers using the Palomar Observatory discovered Makemake, a distant dwarf planet in the Kuiper Belt. Smaller than Pluto yet bright enough to be seen with mid‑sized telescopes, Makemake hinted that our Solar System’s outskirts are more crowded, icy, and myth‑rich than once imagined.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: A newly found icy world drifting slowly in the Kuiper Belt resonates with the unhurried, contemplative orbit of “Slow Planet.”

published.fact_text: Explorer 1, the first satellite of the United States, silently re‑entered Earth's atmosphere on March 31, 1970, after more than 12 years in orbit. Launched during the early space race, it had helped reveal Earth's Van Allen radiation belts before burning up like a shooting star returning home.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

published.match_reason: A pioneering artificial star completing its arc suits a “cosmic” episode about machines, futurism, and orbits that still groove.

published.fact_text: On March 31, 1761, a powerful earthquake estimated at magnitude 8.5 shook the Atlantic off Lisbon, only six years after a previous disaster had devastated the city. The quake and ensuing tsunami rippled across coastlines and frightened sailors at sea, reminding 18th‑century Europe that the Earth beneath their feet could behave like a restless ocean.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A massive quake and tsunami evoke violent, sudden 'weather' of the Earth itself, balanced here by an episode about storms passing and resilience after upheaval.

published.fact_text: In 1931, a devastating earthquake shattered the Nicaraguan capital of Managua, killing around 2,000 people and leveling much of the city. The disaster not only reshaped Managua’s streets and skyline, but also its politics, as reconstruction became tightly bound to the country’s emerging authoritarian rule.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: Both the earthquake and the episode confront overwhelming natural forces; the orca journey’s cinematic calm offers a reflective counterpoint to seismic chaos.

published.fact_text: On March 31, 2023, a historic tornado outbreak tore across the American Midwest and South, spawning dozens of violent twisters in a matter of hours. Entire neighborhoods were reshaped overnight, turning weather radar signatures into very real scars on the landscape and raising urgent questions about climate, preparedness, and resilience.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: A sudden, chaotic outbreak contrasts with an episode about slow, incremental learning—inviting thoughts on how societies can learn from extreme weather and adapt over time.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1931, a Transcontinental & Western Air Fokker F‑10 crashed near Bazaar, Kansas, killing all eight people on board, including legendary Notre Dame football coach Knute Rockne. The tragedy shocked the U.S. so deeply that it accelerated improvements in aircraft design, maintenance, and regulation, reshaping commercial aviation safety.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A fatal flight that reshaped travel safety is balanced with a gentler story of another kind of journey—by sea—where shared movement and vulnerability become part of the adventure.

published.fact_text: In 1854, Commodore Matthew C. Perry of the U.S. Navy signed the Convention of Kanagawa with the Tokugawa shogunate, opening the Japanese ports of Shimoda and Hakodate to American ships. This seemingly modest agreement cracked open over two centuries of near isolation and set Japan on a dramatic course of modernization and cultural exchange.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: A treaty that opened ports and minds parallels an episode about crossing borders, travel, and the subtle perspective shifts that follow.

published.fact_text: On March 31, 1814, the armies of the Sixth Coalition marched into Paris after Napoleon’s forces collapsed, marking the first occupation of the French capital in centuries. For Parisians, the fall of the seemingly invincible emperor felt like waking from a long, militarized dream into a fragile and uncertain new Europe.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: The occupation of Paris and the collapse of imperial power pair with an episode steeped in political rage, resistance, and the sound of empires being challenged.

published.fact_text: In 1942, during World War II, Japanese forces invaded Christmas Island, a remote phosphate‑rich outpost in the Indian Ocean. The tiny island, better known today for its red crab migrations, briefly became a strategic pawn in a global conflict that reached even the most isolated coral and limestone.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: A remote island drawn into wartime strategy mirrors Tuareg lands caught between powers—both stories of far‑flung places whose quiet landscapes hide histories of occupation and resistance.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1992, the USS Missouri, last active battleship of the United States Navy, was decommissioned in Long Beach, California. Famous as the site of Japan’s formal surrender in 1945, the ship retired as a floating relic of an era when armored giants with massive guns ruled the seas.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

published.match_reason: A battleship’s final bow echoes with themes of discipline, combat, and physicality, which resonate with an episode immersed in boxing, training, and the art of controlled force.

published.fact_text: On March 31, 1933, the United States established the Civilian Conservation Corps, a New Deal program that put millions of young men to work restoring forests, building trails, and preventing erosion. Their labor reshaped national parks and wilderness areas, leaving stone cabins, planted trees, and terraced hillsides that still quietly support ecosystems today.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

published.match_reason: A program that put people outdoors rebuilding landscapes pairs with an episode steeped in sun, nature, and the slow, restorative rhythms of an "endless" summer.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1901, Antonín Dvořák's opera "Rusalka" premiered in Prague, bringing to life a Slavic water nymph who sacrifices her voice and place in the natural world for human love. Its haunting "Song to the Moon" turned a local fairy tale into a timeless meditation on longing, transformation, and the cost of leaving one’s element.

episode.title: Sedna FM #04 - Queen of Spades
episode.description: Tuning in from the planet of irrational love. Exploring love's most vulnerable side — the kind that awakens the child within us. A diverse music selection ranging from electric blues to downtempo, soul, and jazz. A tribute to the feeling that has moved artists to create the most beautiful works. Love makes us vulnerable, and there is no art without vulnerability.
songs: Alabama Shakes - Gimme All Your Love
Queens of the stone age - Make it wit chu
Etta James - I'd Rather Go Blind
Eric Clapton - Have you ever loved a woman

published.match_reason: An opera about doomed, self‑sacrificing love belongs with a deep‑dive episode on love’s most vulnerable, irrational corners.

published.fact_text: On March 31, 1913, a concert in Vienna featuring works by Schoenberg, Berg, Zemlinsky, and Webern provoked such outrage that it erupted into fistfights and had to be stopped—earning the nickname "Skandalkonzert" (scandal concert). The atonal, dissonant music shocked audiences used to lush Romantic harmonies, marking a noisy turning point in musical modernism.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

published.match_reason: A riot over shocking new harmonies finds its modern echo in the cultural backlash and fascination around Auto‑Tune, another technology that rewired what music could sound like.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1930, Hollywood adopted the Motion Picture Production Code, better known as the Hays Code, imposing strict moral rules on film content for nearly four decades. Filmmakers suddenly had to imply desire instead of showing it, turning censorship into a strange engine for creativity, subtext, and cinematic innuendo.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: Rules about what we can see on screens in the 1930s resonate with a modern reflection on how screens shape, restrict, and mediate our lives.

published.fact_text: On March 31, 1717, Bishop Benjamin Hoadly delivered a sermon titled "The Nature of the Kingdom of Christ" before King George I, arguing that no earthly church had the right to wield absolute spiritual authority. The sermon ignited the Bangorian Controversy, a fierce pamphlet war over power, conscience, and who gets to interpret the divine.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

published.match_reason: A bishop publicly challenging institutional authority mirrors an episode about breaking out of boxes, labels, and imposed identities.

published.fact_text: In 307, Constantine—future ruler of a reunified Roman Empire—divorced his first partner Minervina and married Fausta, daughter of the retired emperor Maximian. The union was less about romance and more about realpolitik, weaving Constantine into a powerful imperial dynasty through family ties that would later end in betrayal and tragedy.

episode.title: Sedna FM #06 - Alters of an Ego
episode.description: What if you could step outside yourself and become someone freer, bolder, or completely unexpected? Exploring the artistry of musicians who craft alter-egos and parallel realities to experiment with new dimensions of their identity. It's not about hiding who they are — it's about expanding who they can be.
songs: The Fireman - Strawberries Oceans Ships Forest

published.match_reason: Constantine’s strategic shift in alliances and public persona—husband, dynast, eventual Christian emperor—parallels an episode about reinventing identity and adopting new roles.

published.fact_text: On March 31, 1521, Ferdinand Magellan and about fifty of his men came ashore at Limasawa to celebrate what is recorded as the first Catholic Mass in the Philippines. The ceremony fused European ritual with a remote island setting, foreshadowing centuries of cultural, religious, and political entanglement across the archipelago.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: A seafaring expedition weaving faith and culture onto distant shores fits with a boat‑themed episode about maritime routes, songs, and the mixing of coastal worlds.

At just 23, Selena was on the verge of crossing from Spanish‑language fame into global pop stardom, and her death turned her music into a powerful, enduring symbol of Latina identity and loss.
